title: Fermion-doubling problem in Chiral discretizations of Quantum field theory: Definitive proof, Fixing, and Computation of two-point correlation function
comment: 26 pages. 14 pages for the body of the article. 12 pages of appendices. 3 figures related to the body of the article. 2 figures in the appendices
summary: We give the definitive proof that the Dirac Quantum Cellular Automaton (QCA) used for both quantum simulation and algorithmic foundations of Quantum Field Theory (QFT), and especially of Quantum Electrodynamics (QED), as put forward in References https://doi.org/10.1007/s11128-019-2555-4 and https://doi.org/10.22331/q-2023-11-08-1179, does exhibit Fermion Doubling (FD), albeit thrice as less severe as discrete-time standard Lattice Gauge Theories (LGTs) -- as shown in Reference arXiv:2505.07900 -- , which are naive regarding the spacetime discretization of differential operators acting on fermionic fields. The proof is done for the $(1 + 1)$D Dirac-QCA model. We show that the (one-time-step) two-point correlation function, also called Green's function (GF), of the Dirac QCA, is of astonishing simplicity, which is in contrast with the GF of the Dirac equation. We also compare, both qualitatively and quantitatively, this Dirac QCA to the continuous-time-LGT spatial discretization of Dirac fermions regarding how well these two lattice models approximate their naive continuum limit -- which is nothing but the Dirac equation -- even when far away from that limit, a situation which must be considered because of experimental limitations in quantum simulation -- : the Dirac QCA is better for ultrarelativistic regimes, whereas continuous-time LGT is better for non-relativistic regimes. In a second part of this work, we compute the GF of the FD-fixed model put forward in the last cited reference, called Flavored Dirac QCA (FQCA) -- which staggers an extra, artificial flavor \emph{only}, on a diamond spacetime lattice, and does not stagger chirality as staggered fermions in usual LGT. The structure of this FQCA two-point correlation function is of extreme simplicity, and can be expressed in a very simple manner in terms of the four chiral components of the FD-suffering, original-model GF.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a definitive proof of Fermion Doubling (FD) in the Dirac Quantum Cellular Automaton (QCA) model, which is less severe than in standard Lattice Gauge Theories. It analyzes and compares the two-point correlation functions (Green's functions) of the Dirac QCA and continuous-time LGT to their continuum limit, the Dirac equation. The paper also presents a Flavored Dirac QCA (FQCA) that fixes FD, with a simple structure for its two-point correlation function.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the issue of Fermion Doubling in the context of quantum simulation and the algorithmic foundations of Quantum Field Theory, specifically in Quantum Electrodynamics. The goal is to improve the accuracy of lattice models in approximating the continuum limit, the Dirac equation, under experimental limitations.
AI.method: The method involves theoretical analysis and computation of the Green's functions for the Dirac QCA and continuous-time LGT. The authors also develop and analyze a modified model, the Flavored Dirac QCA, which is designed to alleviate the Fermion Doubling problem.
AI.result: The results show that the Dirac QCA exhibits a less severe form of Fermion Doubling compared to discrete-time Lattice Gauge Theories. The Green's function of the Dirac QCA is simpler than that of the Dirac equation. The comparison between the Dirac QCA and continuous-time LGT indicates that the former is more suitable for ultrarelativistic regimes, while the latter is better for non-relativistic regimes. The Flavored Dirac QCA has a very simple structure for its two-point correlation function, which is expressed in terms of the chiral components of the original model's GF.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the Dirac QCA is an improved model over traditional Lattice Gauge Theories for simulating fermions, as it suffers from a milder form of Fermion Doubling. The Flavored Dirac QCA further refines the model, providing a clear and simple solution to the Fermion Doubling problem, making it a promising approach for quantum simulations in both relativistic and non-relativistic contexts.

title: LQCDMaster: Agentic Scientific Computing for Lattice Quantum Chromodynamics Research
summary: Lattice quantum chromodynamics (LQCD) provides a first-principles framework for computing hadronic observables, but its practical use remains limited by the substantial expertise required to turn research motivation into reliable computing workflows. Here we present \textsc{LQCDMaster}, a tool-augmented, skill-guided and domain-specialized scientific computing agent that converts natural-language LQCD research tasks into executable PyQUDA computing workflows, including measurement scripts, job-submission artifacts, execution logs and numerical outputs. The system combines agentic planning, expert-annotated LQCD skills and a deterministic Wick-contraction tool to constrain the algebraically fragile components of code generation. We evaluate \textsc{LQCDMaster} on a benchmark at the forefront of scientific research, comprising 70 LQCD computing tasks, with observables covering local and nonlocal two-point functions, Wilson loops, meson and baryon three-point functions. The generated workflows exactly reproduce expert-written implementations in 63 of 70 tasks at machine precision, with three additional discrepancies attributable to convention mismatches. Across representative observables, the agent reduces implementation time from hours to minutes while preserving end-to-end numerical validation. Further, we present a typical case of \textsc{LQCDMaster}-driven exploration: a lattice computation of light-cone distribution amplitudes with diagonal Wilson-line, a quantity accessible with standard methods but never before computed, and computation of the spectrum of proton, deuteron, triton, hyperon, hyperdeuteron and hypertriton. This work pioneers the paradigm of agentic scientific computing by automating the end-to-end scientific computing workflows in lattice QCD research, lowering its barrier and facilitating the exploration and verification of non-standard scientific ideas.

AI.tldr: LQCDMaster is a scientific computing agent that automates the conversion of natural-language LQCD research tasks into executable PyQUDA computing workflows, reducing implementation time and enabling new explorations in lattice QCD. It accurately reproduces expert-written code for a wide range of observables and facilitates the computation of previously unexplored quantities.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to address the significant expertise barrier associated with Lattice Quantum Chromodynamics (LQCD) computations, which limits its practical use. The authors aim to make LQCD more accessible by automating the process of turning research ideas into reliable computing workflows, thus facilitating the exploration and verification of non-standard scientific ideas within the field.
AI.method: The method introduced in the paper is the LQCDMaster, a tool-augmented, skill-guided, and domain-specialized computing agent. It translates natural-language LQCD research tasks into executable PyQUDA computing workflows. The system uses agentic planning, expert-annotated LQCD skills, and a deterministic Wick-contraction tool to ensure accurate code generation. The performance of LQCDMaster was evaluated on 70 LQCD computing tasks, covering various types of observables.
AI.result: LQCDMaster successfully generated workflows that exactly matched expert-written implementations for 63 out of 70 tasks, with discrepancies in the remaining cases due to convention mismatches. The agent significantly reduced the implementation time from hours to minutes while maintaining numerical accuracy. Additionally, it enabled the computation of light-cone distribution amplitudes and the spectrum of several particles, including some that had not been computed before.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion of the paper is that LQCDMaster pioneers the paradigm of agentic scientific computing in the field of Lattice QCD, making it easier for researchers to perform complex calculations and explore new scientific ideas. The automation of end-to-end scientific computing workflows reduces the entry barrier for LQCD, allowing for more efficient and accurate research.
